greek_head: Εἰς Διονυσίου τοῦ γραμματιστοῦ εἰσῆλθον καὶ εἶδον αὐτόθι τῶν τε νέων τοὺς ἐπιεικεστάτους δοκοῦντας εἶναι τὴν
greek_tokens: eis dionusion o grammatisths eiserxomai kai eidon autoqi o te neos o epieikhs dokew eimi o idea kai pathr eudokimos kai outos erasths tugxanw oun duo o meirakion erizw peri ostis de ou sfodra katakouw fainw mentoi h peri anacagorhs
english_head: I entered the grammar school of the teacher Dionysius, and saw there the young men who are accounted the most comely in form and of distinguished family, and their lovers. Now it chanced that two of the young people were disputing, but about what, I did not clearly overhear: it appeared, however, that they were disputing either about Anaxagoras or about Oenopides; at any rate, they appeared to be drawing circles,

greek_head: ἢ περὶ Οἰνοπίδου ἐρίζειν κύκλους γοῦν γράφειν ἐφαινέσθην καὶ ἐγκλίσεις τινὰς ἐμιμοῦντο τοῖν χεροῖν ἐπικλίνοντε καὶ
greek_tokens: h peri oinopidhs erizw kuklos goun grafw fainw kai egklisis tis mimeomai o xeir epiklinw kai mala spoudazw agos kaqhmai gar para o erasths o eteros autos kinew oun autos o agkwn arow ostis pote outws spoudazw o meirakion eimi kai eipon eimi pou megas tis kai kalos eimi peri o tosoutos spoudh poiew eimi o de eipon poios fhmi megas kai kalos adolesxew men oun outos ge peri o metewros kai fluarew filosofew
english_head: and they were imitating certain inclinations with their arms, bending to it and taking it most earnestly. Then I—for I was sitting beside the lover of one of the pair—nudged him with my elbow and asked him what on earth the two youngsters were so earnest about, and I said: Is it then something great and fine, in which they are so earnestly immersed? Great and fine, indeed! he replied: why, these fellows are prating about the heavenly bodies, and babbling philosophy.

greek_head: Καὶ ἐγὼ θαυμάσας αὐτοῦ τὴν ἀπόκρισιν εἶπον Ὦ νεανία αἰσχρὸν δοκεῖ σοι εἶναι τὸ φιλοσοφεῖν ἢ τί οὕτως χαλεπῶς
greek_tokens: kai egw qaumazw autos o apokrisls eipon eimi neanihs aisxros dokew su eimi o filosofew h tis outws xalepos legw kai o eteros pimplhmi gar kaqhmai tugxanw autos anterasths eimi aekwn egw te eromai ekeinos apokrinw ou pros sew ge fhmi eimi swkraths poiew o kai aneromai outos ei aisxros hgeomai filosofia eimi h ou oida outos ostis traxhlizw kai empiplhmi kai kaqeudw pas o bios diatelew wste su tis autos oiomai apokrinw alla h ostis aisxros eimi filosofia
english_head: Then I, surprised at his answer, said: Young man, do you consider philosophizing to be shameful? Else, why do you speak so sharply? Then the other youth—for he chanced to be sitting near him, as his rival in love—when he heard my question and his rival’s answer, said: You do yourself no good, Socrates, by pressing this fellow with a further question, as to whether he considers philosophizing to be shameful. Do you not know that he has spent the whole of his life in practising the neckhold, and s

greek_head: Ἦν δὲ οὗτος μὲν τοῖν ἐρασταῖν περὶ μουσικὴν διατετριφώς ὁ δ' ἕτερος ὃν ἐλοιδόρει περὶ γυμναστικήν καί
greek_tokens: eimi de outos men o erasths peri mousikh diatribw o de eteros os loidorew peri gumnastikos kai egw dokew xrh o men eteros afihmi o erwtaw ostis oude autos prospoiew peri logos empeiros eimi alla peri ergnumi o de sofos prospoiew eimi dierwtaw ina kai ei tis dunamai para autos wfelew eipon oun ostis eis koinos men o erwthma arow ei de su oiomai ode kallion an apokrinw sos erwtaw o autos osper kai outos ei dokew su o filosofew kalos eimi h ou
english_head: Now this one of the two lovers had spent his time on humane studies, whereas the other, whom he was abusing, had spent his on athletics. So I decided that I had best relinquish the other, whom I had been questioning, since he did not even himself set up to be experienced in words, but only in deeds; and that I should interrogate the one who set up to be wiser, in order that so far as I was able I might get some benefit from him. I said therefore: I addressed my question to both in common; but if

greek_head: Σχεδὸν οὖν ταῦτα λεγόντων ἡμῶν ἐπακούσαντε τὼ μειρακίω ἐσιγησάτην καὶ αὐτὼ παυσαμένω τῆς ἔριδος ἡμῶν
greek_tokens: sxedon oun outos legw egw epakouw o meirakion sigaw kai autos pauw o eris egw akroaths gignomai kai ostis men e erasths pasxw ou oida autos de oun ekplhssw aei gar pote upo o neos te kai kalh ekplhssw dokew mentoi egw kai o eteros ou hsswn egw agwnia ou meis alla apokrinw ge egw kai mala filotimos opote gar su fhmi eimi swkraths
english_head: Then the two striplings, overhearing us speak somewhat like this, were silent, and ceasing from their own contention they became listeners to ours. What their lovers’ sensations were, I do not know, but I myself, at any rate, was staggered; for every time I am staggered by handsome young people. It seemed to me, however, that my young friend too was in as great a flutter as myself; but nevertheless he answered me in a most ambitious spirit: Why, of course, Socrates, he said, if I should ever con

greek_head: τὸ φιλοσοφεῖν αἰσχρὸν ἡγησαίμην εἶναι οὐδ' ἂν ἄνθρωπον νομίσαιμι ἐμαυτὸν εἶναι οὐδ' ἄλλον τὸν οὕτω διακείμενον
greek_tokens: o filosofew aisxros hgeomai eimi oude an anqrwpos nomizw emautou eimi oude allos o outws diakeimai endeiknumi eis o anterasths kai legos megas o fwnew ina autos katakouw o paidikon kai egw eipon kalos ara dokew su o filosofew panu men oun fhmi tis oun egw fhmi eimi dokew su oios su eimi oida pragma otioun eite kalos eite aisxros eimi o mh oida tis o arxh ostis eimi ou fhmi
english_head: nor anyone else either who was disposed to think so. Here he pointed to his rival lover, and spoke with a loud voice, in order that his favorite might hear every word. Then I remarked: So philosophizing seems to you to be honorable? Quite so, he said. Well now, I said; does it seem to you possible to know whether anything is honorable or shameful without knowing what it is fundamentally? No, he said.

greek_head: Οἶσθ' ἄρα ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὅτι ἔστιν τὸ φιλοσοφεῖν Πάνυ γε ἔφη
greek_tokens: oistos ara eimi de egw ostis eimi o filosofew panu ge fhmi tis oun eimi fhmi egw tis de allos ge h kata o solwn solos gar pou eipon ghraskw de aei polus didaskw kai egw dokew outws aei xrh eis ge tis manqanw o mellw filosofew kai neos eimi kai presbus ina ws pleistos en o bios maqh kai egw o men proteros dokew tis eipon epeita pws ennoew arow autos ei o filosofia polumaqia hgeomai
english_head: Then do you know, I went on, what philosophizing is? Certainly I do, said he. Then what is it? I asked. Why, just what Solon called it; you know it was Solon who said:And ever, as I older grow, I learn yet more and more;Solon Fr. 18—and I agree with him that a man who intends to philosophize should in this way be ever learning something or other, whether he be younger or older, in order that he may learn as many things as possible in his life. Now at first I felt there was something in his reply

greek_head: Κἀκεῖνος Πάνυ ἔφη Ἡγῇ δὲ δὴ καλὸν εἶναι μόνον τὴν φιλοσοφίαν ἢ καὶ
greek_tokens: ekeinos panu fhmi hgeomai de dh kalos eimi monos o filosofia h kai agaqos eimi de egw kai agaqos fhmi panu poteros oun en filosofia tis outos idios enoraw h kai en o allos outws su dokew exw oios filogumnastia ou monos hgeomai kalos eimi alla kai agaqos h ou o de kai mala eirwnikos eipon duo pros men ode egw erw ostis oudeteros pros mentoi sos eimi swkraths
english_head: To which he answered: Certainly. And do you consider philosophy to be merely honorable, I asked, or good as well? Good as well, he said: very much so. Then do you observe this as peculiar to philosophy, or do you find it similarly in everything else? For example, do you consider the love of athletics to be not merely honorable, but good as well, or do you not? Whereupon he, most slily, gave a double answer: To him my statement must be neither; but to you, Socrates, I acknowledge it to be both ho

greek_head: ὁμολογῶ καὶ καλὸν εἶναι καὶ ἀγαθόν ἡγοῦμαι γὰρ ὀρθῶς Ἠρώτησα οὖν ἐγώ Ἆρ' οὖν καὶ ἐν τοῖς γυμνασίοις τὴν
greek_tokens: omologew kai kalos eimi kai agaqos hgeomai gar orqos erwtaw oun egw ara oun kai en o gumnasion o poluponia filogumnastia hgeomai eimi ekeinos fhmi panu ge wsper ge kai en o filosofew o polumaqia filosofia hgeomai eimi agos eipon hgeomai de dh o filogumnastew allos tis epiqumew h outos ostis poihsis autos eu exw o swma outos fhmi eimi oun e polus ponos o swma eimi de egw poiew eu exw
english_head: for I consider this the right view. Then I asked him: Well now, in athletics, do you consider that much exercise is love of athletics? To which he replied: Certainly, just as in philosophizing I consider much learning to be philosophy. Then I said: And do you then consider that the lovers of athletics desire anything else than that which will cause them to be in good bodily condition? Only that, he replied. And does much exercise, I asked, cause them to be in good bodily condition?

greek_head: Πῶς γὰρ ἄν ἔφη ἀπό γε ὀλίγων πόνων τὸ σῶμά τις εὖ ἔχοι
greek_tokens: pws gar an fhmi apo ge oligos ponos o swma tis eu exw kai egw dokew hdh entauqa kinhteos eimi o filogumnasths ina egw boaw dia o empeiria o gumnastikos epeita arow autos su de dh tis sigaw egw eimi lwstos outos outos legw h kai sos dokew e anqrwpos eu o swma exw apo o polus ponos h apo o metrios egw men eimi swkraths fhmi oiomai o legw dh outos kan us gignwskw ostis e metrios ponos eu poiew exw o
english_head: Yes, for how, he replied, could one be in good bodily condition through little exercise? Here I felt it was time to stir up the lover of athletics, in order that he might give me the support of his athletic experience; so I proceeded to ask him: And you then, pray, why are you silent, excellent sir, while your friend here is speaking thus? Do you agree that men are in good bodily condition through much exercise, or is it rather through moderate exercise? For my part, Socrates, he said, I thought

greek_head: σώματα πόθεν δὴ οὐχὶ ἄνδρα γε ἄγρυπνόν τε καὶ ἄσιτον καὶ ἀτριβῆ τὸν τράχηλον ἔχοντα καὶ λεπτὸν ὑπὸ μεριμνῶν
greek_tokens: swma pinw dh ou anhr ge agrupnos te kai asitos kai atribhs o traxhlos exw kai lepton upo merimna kai autos outos eipon ezomai o meirakion kai epigelaw o de eteros eruqriaw kai egw eipon tis oun su hdh sugxwrew mhte polus mhte oligos ponos eu poiew exw o swma o anqrwpos alla o metrios h diamaxh duo eimi egw peri o logos
english_head: as the saying is—would have known that moderate exercise causes them to be in good bodily condition, so why should not a fellow who is sleepless and unfed, with unchafed neck and slender, care-worn frame! And when he had said this the boys were delighted and laughed their approval, while the other lover blushed. Then I said to him: Well, do you now concede that it is neither much, nor little, but moderate exercise that causes men to be in good bodily condition? Or do you bid defiance to the two

greek_head: Κἀκεῖνος Πρὸς μὲν τοῦτον ἔφη κἂν πάνυ ἡδέως διαγωνισαίμην καὶ εὖ οἶδ' ὅτι ἱκανὸς ἂν γενοίμην βοηθῆσαι
greek_tokens: ekeinos pros men outos fhmi kan panu hdews diagwnizomai kai eu oida ostis ikanos an gignomai bohqew o upoqesis os upotiqhmi kai ei outos eti faulos upotiqhmi oudeis gar eimi pros mentoi sos oudeis deomai para doca filoneikew alla omologew mh o polus alla o metrios gumnasion o euecia empoiew o anqrwpos tis de o sition o metrios h o polus fhmi egw kai o sition omologew
english_head: To which he answered: Against him I should be only too glad to fight it out, and I am certain I should prove able to support the theory I have put forward, even had I put forward a weaker one; for he is naught. But with you I do not aim at winning an unscrupulous success; and so I admit that not a great but a moderate amount of athletics causes good condition in men. And what of food? Moderate or much? I asked. The same applied to food, he admitted.

greek_head: Ἔτι δὲ κἀγὼ προσηνάγκαζον αὐτὸν ὁμολογεῖν καὶ τἆλλα πάντα τὰ περὶ τὸ σῶμα ὠφελιμώτατα εἶναι τὰ μέτρια ἀλλὰ
greek_tokens: eti de agos prosanagkazw autos omologew kai allos pas o peri o swma wfelimos eimi o metrios alla mh o polus mhde o oligos kai egw omologew o metrios tis de fhmi o peri o yuxh o metrios wfelew h o ametros o prosferw o metrios fhmi oukoun eis o prosferw yuxw eimi kai o maqhma omologew kai outos ara o metrios wfelew alla ou o polus sumfhmi
english_head: Then I went on and tried to compel him also to admit that everything else connected with the body when most beneficial, was the moderate thing, not the much or the little; and he admitted that it was the moderate thing. And now, I said, as regards the soul; are moderate or immoderate things beneficial, as adjuncts of it? Moderate things, he replied. And are studies among the adjuncts of the soul? He admitted they were. So among these also it is the moderate that are beneficial, and not the much?

greek_head: Τίνα οὖν ἐρόμενοι ἂν δικαίως ἐροίμεθα ὁποῖοι μέτριοι πόνοι καὶ σιτία πρὸς τὸ σῶμά ἐστιν
greek_tokens: tis oun eromai an dikaios eromai opoios metrios ponos kai sition pros o swma eimi omologew men trew eimi ostis iatros h paidotribhs tis de an peri sperma spora oposos metrios kai outos o gewrgos omologew tis de peri maqhma eis yuxh futeusis te kai spora erwtaw dikaios an eromai oposos kai opoios metrios
english_head: And whom should we ask about the moderate measure in the sowing of seed? In that matter, we agreed, it must be a farmer. And whom should we be justified in asking as to the moderate degree and kind, in regard to the sowing and planting of studies in the soul? At this point we all began to be full of perplexity;

greek_head: Τοὐντεῦθεν ἤδη ἀπορίας μεστοὶ ἦμεν ἅπαντες κἀγὼ προσπαίζων αὐτοὺς ἠρόμην Βούλεσθε ἔφην ἐπειδὴ ἡμεῖς
greek_tokens: enteuqen hdh aporia mestos eimi apas agos prospaizw autos arow boulomai fhmi epei egw en aporia eimi eramai outos o meirakion h isos aisxunw wsper fhmi o mnhsthr omhros mh aciow eimi tis allos ostis enteinw o tocon epei oun egw dokew aqumew pros o logos allh peiraw skopaw kai eipon poios de malista atta topazw eimi o maqhma o deomai o filosofew manqanw epei ou pas oude polus
english_head: then I, mocking at them, asked: Do you mind, since we are in perplexity, if we ask these boys here? or perhaps we are ashamed, as Homer said the suitors were, and do not think it fit there should be someone else who will string the bow? Then, as it seemed to me that they were losing their zeal for the argument, I tried to pursue the inquiry in another way, and said: But what, as nearly as we can guess, are the kinds of learning which the philosopher should learn, since he is not to learn all thi

greek_head: Ὑπολαβὼν οὖν ὁ σοφώτερος εἶπεν ὅτι Κάλλιστα ταῦτ' εἴη τῶν μαθημάτων καὶ προσήκοντα ἀφ' ὧν ἂν πλείστην
greek_tokens: upolambanw oun o sofos eipon ostis kallista outos eaw o maqhma kai proshkw apo os an pleistos doca exw tis eis filosofia pleistos de an exw doca ei dokew o texnh empeiros eimi pas ei de mh ws pleistos ge kai malista o aciologos manqanw autos outos o proshkw o eleuqeros manqanw osos sunesis exw mh osos xeirourgia ara oun outws legw fhmi egw wsper en o tektonikos
english_head: At this the wiser one interjected: The finest and most suitable kinds of learning are those which will bring him the most reputation for philosophy; and he will get most reputation if he appears well versed in all the arts, or if not in all, in as many of them, and those the most considerable, as he can, by learning so much of them as befits a free man to learn, that is, what belongs to the understanding rather than the handiwork of each. Well now, do you mean, I asked, in the same way as in car

greek_head: καὶ γὰρ ἐκεῖ τέκτονα μὲν ἂν πρίαιο πέντε ἢ ἓξ μνῶν ἄκρον ἀρχιτέκτονα δὲ οὐδ' ἂν μυρίων δραχμῶν ὀλίγοι γε μὴν κἂν
greek_tokens: kai gar ekei tektwn men an priamai pente h ec mna akron arxitektwn de oude an murw draxmh oligos ge meis kan en pas o ellhn gignomai ara mh tis toioutos legw kai os aekwn egw sugxwrew kai autos legw toioutos arow de autos ei ou adunatos eaw duo monos texnh outws manqanw o autos mh ostis polus kai megas o de mh outws egw fhmi upolambanw eimi swkraths ws legw ostis deomai ekastos o texnh o filosofew efisthmi
english_head: or six minae, but a first-rate architect cannot be got for even ten thousand drachmae; few such, indeed, could be found throughout the whole of Greece. Is it something of this sort that you mean? When he heard me say this, he admitted that something like this was what he himself meant. I next asked him if it was not impossible for the same person to learn in this way merely two of the arts, not to speak of many or the principal ones; to which he replied: Do not conceive me, Socrates,

greek_head: ἀκριβῶς ὥσπερ αὐτὸν τὸν τὴν τέχνην ἔχοντα ἀλλ' ὡς εἰκὸς ἄνδρα ἐλεύθερόν τε καὶ πεπαιδευμένον ἐπακολουθῆσαί
greek_tokens: akribhs wsper autos o o texnh exw alla ws eoika anhr eleuqeros te kai paideuw epakolouqew te o legw upo o dhmiourgew oios su eimi diaferontws o pareimi kai autos sumballw gnwmh wste dokew xarieis eimi kai sofos o aei pareimi en o legw te kai prassw peri o texnh agos eti gar autos amfignoew o logos ostis boulomai
english_head: to be stating that the philosopher must have accurate knowledge of each of the arts, like the actual adept in any of them; I mean only so far as may be expected of a free and educated man: that is, he should be able to follow the explanations of the craftsman more readily than the rest of the company, and to contribute an opinion of his own which will make him appear the cleverest and most accomplished of the company who may at any time be present at some verbal or practical exposition of the ar

greek_head: Ἆρ' ἐννοῶ ἔφην οἷον λέγεις τὸν φιλόσοφον ἄνδρα δοκεῖς γάρ μοι λέγειν οἷοι ἐν τῇ ἀγωνίᾳ εἰσὶν οἱ πένταθλοι πρὸς
greek_tokens: ara ennoew fhmi oios legw o filosofos anhr dokew gar egw legw oios en o agwnia eimi e pentaqlos pros o dromeus h o palaisth kai gar ekeinos outos men leipw kata o outos aqlon kai deuteros eimi pros outos o de allos aqlhths proteros kai nikaw autos taxa an isos toioutos tis legos kai o filosofew apergazomai o epithdeuw outos o epithdeuma o men
english_head: For you seem to me to mean someone like the all-round athletes in contest with the runners or the wrestlers: the former yield, you know, to the latter in their particular exercises, and are their inferiors in these, but are superior to the usual sort of athletes and beat them. I daresay it may be something of this sort that you would suggest as the effect produced by philosophy on those who make it their pursuit: they yield to those who are first-rate

greek_head: πρώτων εἰς σύνεσιν περὶ τὰς τέχνας ἐλλείπεσθαι τὰ δευτερεῖα δ' ἔχοντας τῶν ἄλλων περιεῖναι καὶ οὕτως γίγνεσθαι
greek_tokens: proteros eis sunesis peri o texnh elleipw o deutereia de exw o allos perieimi kai outws gignomai peri pas upakros tis anhr o filosofew toioutos tis egw dokew endeiknumi kalos ge egw fhmi eimi swkraths fainw upolambanw o peri o filosofos apeikazw autos o pentaqlon eimi gar atexnos toioutos oios mh douleuw mhdeis pragma mhde eis o akribeia mhdeis diaponew wste dia o o eis outos epimeleia o allos apas
english_head: in an understanding of the arts, but in taking the second place they surpass the rest; and in this way the man who has studied philosophy comes just next to the top in everything. That is the kind of person whom you appear to me to indicate. You are quite right, it seems to me, Socrates, he said, in your conception of the philosopher’s position, with your comparison of him to the all-round athlete. For it is precisely his nature not to be enslaved to any business, or to work out anything exactly

greek_head: ἀπολελεῖφθαι ὥσπερ οἱ δημιουργοί ἀλλὰ πάντων μετρίως ἐφῆφθαι
greek_tokens: apoleipw wsper e dhmiourgos alla pas metrios efaptw meta outos dh o apokrisls egw proqumeomai safhs oida ostis legos punqanomai autos o agaqos poteros xrhsimos h axrhstos eimi upolambanw xrhsimos dhpou eimi swkraths fhmi ara oun eiper e agaqos xrhsimos e ponhros axrhstos omologew tis de o filosofos anhr xrhsimos hgeomai h ou
english_head: as the craftsmen do, but to have a moderate contact with all of them. Well, after this answer I was eager to know clearly what he meant, so I inquired of him whether he conceived of good men as useful or useless. Useful, I should say, Socrates, he replied. Then if good men are useful, are wicked men useless? He agreed that they were. Again, do you consider that philosophers are useful persons or not?

greek_head: Ὁ δὲ ὡμολόγει χρησίμους καὶ πρός γε ἔφη χρησιμωτάτους εἶναι ἡγεῖσθαι
greek_tokens: o de omologew xrhsimos kai pros ge fhmi xrhsimos eimi hgeomai ferw dh gignwskw ei su alhqhs legw pou kai xrhsimos egw eimi e upakros outos dhlos gar ostis ekastos ge o o texnh exw faulos eimi o filosofos omologew ferw dh su eimi de egw ei tuxos h autos asqenew h o filos tis o sew peri os su spoudh megas exis poteros ugeios boulomai ktaomai o upakros ekeinos o filosofos eisagw an eis o oikia h o iatros lambanw
english_head: He agreed that they were useful; nay, more, that he considered they were most useful of all. Come now, let us make out, if what you say is true, where these second-best men are also useful to us: for clearly the philosopher is inferior to any particular adept in the arts. He agreed. Well now, I went on, if you yourself, or one of your friends for whom you feel great concern, should have fallen sick, would you fetch that second-best man into the house with a view to obtaining health, or would you

greek_head: Ἀμφοτέρους ἔγωγ' ἄν ἔφη Μή μοι εἶπον ἐγώ ἀμφοτέρους λέγε ἀλλ' ὁπότερον
greek_tokens: amfoteros egw an fhmi mh egw eipon egw amfoteros legos alla opoteros mallon te kai proteros oudeis an fhmi outos ge amfisbhtew ws ou o iatros kai mallon kai proteros tis de en naias xeimazw poteros an mallon epitrepw sautou te kai o sautou o kubernhths h o filosofos o kubernhths egw oukoun kai allos panth outws ews an tis dhmiourgos eimi ou xrhsimos eimi o filosofos fainw fhmi
english_head: For my part, I should have both, he replied. Please do not say both, I said, but which of the two you would prefer and also summon first. No one, he replied,would make any question but that the doctor should be preferred and also summoned first. And again, if you were in a ship that was making rough weather, to which would you rather entrust yourself and yours, the pilot or the philosopher? I should choose the pilot. And so it will be in everything else: so long as there is some craftsman, the p

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν νῦν ἄχρηστός τις ἡμῖν ἐστιν ὁ φιλόσοφος εἰσὶ γὰρ ἡμῖν ἀεί που δημιουργοί ὡμολογήσαμεν δὲ τοὺς μὲν
greek_tokens: oukoun nun axrhstos tis egw eimi o filosofos eimi gar egw aei pou dhmiourgos omologew de o men agaqos xrhsimos eimi o de moxqhros axrhstos anagkazw omologew tis oun meta outos eromai su h agroikos eimi eromai eromai ostis boulomai oudeis dh fhmi egw zhtew allos h anomologeomai o
english_head: So now we find that the philosopher is a useless person? For I suppose we always have craftsmen; and we have agreed that good men are useful, and bad ones useless. He was obliged to agree to this. Then what follows? Am I to ask you, or will it be too ill-mannered? Ask whatever you please. Well, my aim, I said, is merely to recall our agreements upon

greek_head: εἰρημένα ἔχει δέ πως ὡδί ὡμολογήσαμεν καλὸν εἶναι τὴν φιλοσοφίαν καὶ αὐτοὶ φιλόσοφοι εἶναι τοὺς δὲ φιλοσόφους
greek_tokens: erw exis de pws wdi omologew kalos eimi o filosofia kai autos filosofos eimi o de filosofos agaqos o de agaqos xrhsimos o de ponhros axrhstos auqis de au o filosofos omologew ews an e dhmiourgos eimi axrhstos eimi dhmiourgos de aei eimi ou gar outos omologew panu ge eimi de os omologew ara ws eoika kata ge o sos logos eiper o filosofew eimi peri o texnh episthmwn eimi os su legw o tropos ponhros autos eimi kai
english_head: what has been stated. The matter stands somewhat like this. We agreed that philosophy is an honorable thing, and that philosophers are good; and that good men are useful, and wicked men useless: but then again we agreed that philosophers, so long as we have craftsmen, are useless, and that we always do have craftsmen. Has not all this been agreed? Yes, to be sure, he replied. Then we agreed, it seems, by your account—if philosophizing means having knowledge of the arts in the way you describe—th

greek_head: ἀχρήστους ἕως ἂν ἐν ἀνθρώποις τέχναι ὦσιν ἀλλὰ μὴ οὐχ οὕτως ὦ φίλε ἔχωσι μηδ' ᾖ τοῦτο φιλοσοφεῖν περὶ
greek_tokens: axrhstos ews an en anqrwpos texnh eimi alla mh ou outws eimi filos exw mhde eimi outos filosofew peri o texnh spoudazw oude polupragmonew kuptazw zaw oude polumaqew alla allos tis epei egw oiomai kai oneidos eimi outos kai banausos kalew o peri o texnh spoudazw wde de safhs eimi ei ara alhqhs legos ean outos apokrinw tinw ippos efisthmi
english_head: among mankind. But I expect they are not so really, my friend, and that philosophizing is not just having a concernment in the arts or spending one’s life in meddlesome stooping and prying and accumulation of learning, but something else; because I imagined that this life was actually a disgrace, and that people who concerned themselves with the arts were called sordid. But we shall know more definitely whether this statement of mine is true, if you will answer me this: What men know how to puni

greek_head: κολάζειν ὀρθῶς πότερον οἵπερ βελτίστους ποιοῦσιν ἢ ἄλλοι Οἵπερ βελτίστους
greek_tokens: kolazw orqos poteros e beltistos poiew h allos e beltistos tis de kuwn ou e beltistos efisthmi poiew outos kai kolazw orqos efisthmi nai o autos ara texnh beltistos te poiew kai kolazw orqos fainw egw eimi de os tis de poteros o beltistos te poiew kai kolazw orqos o autos de kai gignwskw o xrhstos kai o moxqhros h eteros tis o autos fhmi eqelw oun kai kata anqrwpos outos omologew
english_head: Is it those who make them into the best horses, or some other men? Those who make them into the best horses. Or again, is it not the men who know how to make dogs into the best dogs that know also how to punish them rightly? Yes. Then it is the same art that makes them into the best dogs and punishes them rightly? It appears so to me, he replied. Again, is the art that makes them into the best ones and punishes them rightly the same as that which knows the good and the bad ones, or is it some ot

greek_head: ἥπερ βελτίστους ἀνθρώπους ποιεῖ ταύτην εἶναι καὶ τὴν κολάζουσαν ὀρθῶς καὶ διαγιγνώσκουσαν τοὺς χρηστούς τε
greek_tokens: o beltistos anqrwpos poiew outos eimi kai o kolazw orqos kai diagignwskw o xrhstos te kai moxqhros panu ge fhmi oukoun kai ostis enos kai polus kai ostis polus kai enos nai kai kaqa ippos dh kai o allos apas outws fhmi tis oun eimi o episthmh ostis o en o polis akolastainw kai paranomew orqos kolazw ou o dikastikos nai eimi allos oun tis kalew kai dikaiosunh h outos ou alla outos
english_head: which makes them into the best men is that which punishes them rightly and distinguishes the good and the bad ones? Certainly, he said. And that which does this to one, does it also to many, and that which does it to many, does it also to one? Yes. And so it is also with horses and everything else? I agree. Then what is the knowledge which rightly punishes the licentious and law-breaking people in our cities? Is it not judicature? Yes. And is it any other art than this that you call justice? No,

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ᾗπερ κολάζουσιν ὀρθῶς ταύτῃ καὶ γιγνώσκουσι τοὺς χρηστοὺς καὶ μοχθηρούς
greek_tokens: oukoun hper kolazw orqos outos kai gignwskw o xrhstos kai moxqhros outos ostis de enos gignwskw kai polus gignwskw nai kai ostis ge polus agnoew kai enos fhmi ei ara ippos eimi agnoew o xrhstos kai ponhros ippos kan eautou agnoew poios tis eimi fhmi kai ei bous eimi agnoew o ponhros kai xrhstos bous kan eautou agnoew poios tis eimi nai fhmi outws dh kai ei kuw omologew
english_head: And that whereby they punish rightly is that whereby they know the good and bad people? It is. And whoever knows one will know many also? Yes. And whoever does not know many will not know one? I agree. Then if one were a horse, and did not know the good and wicked horses, would one not know which sort one was oneself? I think not. And if one were an ox and did not know the wicked and good oxen, would one not know which sort one was oneself? That is so, he said. And so it would be, if one were a

greek_head: Τί δ' ἐπειδὰν ἄνθρωπός τις ὢν ἀγνοῇ τοὺς χρηστοὺς καὶ μοχθηροὺς ἀνθρώπους ἆρ' οὐχ αὑτὸν ἀγνοεῖ πότερον
greek_tokens: tis de epeidan anqrwpos tis eimi agnoew o xrhstos kai moxqhros anqrwpos ara ou eautou agnoew poteros xrhstos eimi h ponhros epei kai autos anqrwpos eimi sugxwrew o de eautou agnoew swfronew eimi h mh swfronew mh swfronew o eautou ara gignwskw eimi swfronew fhmi fhmi outos ara ws eoika o en delfoi gramma parakeleuomai swfrosunos askew kai dikaiosunh eoika o autos de outos kai kolazw orqos efisthmi nai
english_head: Well now, when one is a man, and does not know the good and bad men, one surely cannot know whether one is good or wicked oneself, since one is a man also oneself? He granted this. And is not knowing oneself being temperate, or not being temperate? Not being temperate. So knowing oneself is being temperate? I agree, he said. So this is the message, it seems, of the Delphic inscription—that one is to practise temperance and justice. It seems so. And it is by this same art that we know also how to

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ᾗ μὲν κολάζειν ὀρθῶς ἐπιστάμεθα δικαιοσύνη αὕτη ἐστίν ᾗ δὲ διαγιγνώσκειν καὶ ἑαυτὸν καὶ ἄλλους
greek_tokens: oukoun h men kolazw orqos efisthmi dikaiosunh outos eimi h de diagignwskw kai eautou kai allos swfrosunos eoika fhmi tautos ara eimi kai dikaiosunh kai swfrosunos fainw kai meis outws ge kai o polis eu oikew otan e adikew dikh didhmi alhqhs legw fhmi kai politikos ara autos eimi sundokew tis de otan eis anhr orqos polis dioikew onoma ge outos ou turannos te kai basileus fhmi oukoun basilikos te kai turannikos texnh dioikew outws kai outos ara o autos texnh eimi ekeinos fainw
english_head: is justice, and that whereby we know how to distinguish our own and others’ quality is temperance? It seems so, he said. Then justice and temperance are the same thing? Apparently. And further, it is thus, you know, that cities are well ordered—when the wrongdoers pay the penalty. That is true, he said. Hence this is also statecraft. He concurred. Again, when one man governs a city rightly, is he not called a despot and king? I agree. And he governs by a kingly and despotic art? That is so. And

greek_head: Τί δ' ὅταν εἷς ὢν ἀνὴρ οἰκίαν διοικῇ ὀρθῶς τί ὄνομα τούτῳ ἐστίν οὐκ οἰκονόμος τε καὶ δεσπότης
greek_tokens: tis de otan eis eimi anhr oikia dioikew orqos tis onoma outos eimi ou oikonomos te kai despoths nai poteros oun kai outos dikaiosunh eu an o oikia dioikew h allh tis texnh dikaiosunh eimi ara tautos ws eoika basileus turannos politikos oikonomos despoths swfrwn dikaios kai eis texnh eimi basilikos turannikos politikos despotikos oikonomikos dikaiosunh swfrosunos fainw fhmi outws
english_head: Again, when a man singly governs a house aright, what is he called? Is he not a house-manager and master? Yes. Then would he also govern his house well by justice, or by some other art? By justice. Hence they are all the same, it seems,—king, despot, statesman, house-manager, master, and the temperate man and the just man; and it is all one art,—the kingly, the despotic, the statesman’s, the master’s, the house-manager’s, and justice and temperance. It is so, apparently, he said.

greek_head: Πότερον οὖν τῷ φιλοσόφῳ ὅταν μὲν ἰατρὸς περὶ τῶν καμνόντων τι λέγῃ αἰσχρὸν μήθ' ἕπεσθαι τοῖς λεγομένοις
greek_tokens: poteros oun o filosofos otan men iatros peri o kamnw tis legos aisxros mhte epomai o legw dunamai mhte sumballw mhdeis peri o legw h prassw kai opotan allos tis o dhmiourgew wsautws otan de dikasths h basileus h allos tis os nundi dierxomai ou aisxros peri outos mhte epomai dunamai mhte sumballw peri autos pws de ou aisxros eimi swkraths peri ge tosoutos pragma mhdeis exw sumballw
english_head: Then, if it is disgraceful in the philosopher to be unable, when a doctor speaks about the sick, either to follow his remarks or to contribute anything of his own to what is being said or done, and to be in the same case when any other of the craftsmen speaks, is it not disgraceful that he should be unable, when it is a judge or a king or some other of the persons whom we have just instanced, either to follow their words or contribute anything to their business? It must indeed be disgraceful, So

greek_head: Πότερον οὖν καὶ περὶ ταῦτα λέγωμεν ἔφην πένταθλον αὐτὸν δεῖν εἶναι καὶ ὕπακρον καὶ ταύτης μὲν τὰ δευτερεῖα
greek_tokens: poteros oun kai peri outos legw fhmi pentaqlon autos dew eimi kai upakros kai outos men o deutereia exw pas o filosofos kai axreios eimi ews an outos tis eimi h proteros men o eautou oikia ou allos epitrepteon oude o deutereia en outos ekteon alla autos kolasteos dikazw orqos ei mellw eu oikew autos o oikia sugxwrew dh egw epeita ge dhpou ean e filos autos diaita epitrepw ean o polis tis prostassw diakrinw h dikazw
english_head: Are we then to say, I asked, that in these matters also he is to be an all-round athlete, a second-rate man, taking the second place in all the subjects of this art—he, the philosopher—and is to be useless so long as there is one of these persons; or that, first of all, he is to entrust his own house to nobody else and is not to take the second place in it, but is himself to judge and punish rightly, if his house is to be well managed? He granted me that it must be so. Secondly, I presume, wheth

greek_head: αἰσχρὸν ἐν τούτοις ὦ ἑταῖρε δεύτερον φαίνεσθαι ἢ τρίτον καὶ μὴ οὐχ ἡγεῖσθαι
greek_tokens: aisxros en outos eimi etairos deuteros fainw h tritos kai mh ou hgeomai dokew egw polus ara deomai egw eimi beltistos o filosofew polumaqia te eimi kai o peri o texnh pragmateia eipon de egw outos o men sofos aisxunw o proerew sigaw o de amaqhs fhmi ekeinos eimi kai e allos epainew o erw
english_head: or judge any matter, it is disgraceful for him, my good friend, in such cases, to be found in the second or third place, and not to lead? I agree. Hence we see, my excellent sir, that philosophizing is very far from being much learning and that affair of busying oneself with the arts. On my saying this the cultivated youth was silent, feeling ashamed for what he had said before, while the unlearned one said it was as I stated; and the rest of the company praised the argument.
